text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic left lateral sectionectomy on a sixty-two-year-old female with segment three biliary cystadenoma. The surgical field shows a slightly fatty liver with a cystic lesion in segment three. Ports are positioned, including a ten mm supramedical port and a twelve mm port for the stapler. The round ligament is taken down from the parietal wall, followed by the falciform ligament, and then the right coronary and triangular ligaments. Pneumo dissection is used on the triangular ligament, leading to the coronary ligament. Traction is applied to the left collateral section to fully take down the left coronary ligament, exposing the left side of the bare area of the liver. The IVC and crest of the diaphragm are visible behind, as the left collateral section is reflected above.

The surgeon opens the past flaccida of the aceromentum and enters the space. The accessory left hepatic artery is dissected from the left gastric artery, progressing to the superior surface. Preparation for the Pringle maneuver is shown, using a modified Huygens technique where a grasper is passed through the foramina of Winslow, and a cut Foley's catheter is positioned for potential tightening and clipping. Rubber band technique is employed for dynamic retraction during parenchymal transection, with sutures applied for stabilization.

The surgeon begins parenchymal transection slightly to the left of the falciform ligament using harmonic and bipolar forceps, ensuring the field remains dry. The active jaw of the harmonic device is inserted into the liver parenchyma, followed by slow closure of the inactive jaw to transect the tissue. Crossing branches of the left hepatic vein and portal vein system are visible superiorly, while segment two and three plates appear inferiorly. A left hepatic venous branch traversing between segments three and four is hooked with a silk suture and secured with hemlock clips. The parenchymal transection continues slowly, using bipolar cautery for minor bleeding and small vessels. As the dissection progresses posteriorly, another branch, possibly segment IV A portal vein, is observed, and the segment II and III pedicle is prepared for stapling.

The surgeon carefully staples the segment two and three portal vein plate, avoiding the umbilical portion of the left portal vein. Approximately six centimeters of parenchyma is lifted, and dissection progresses posteriorly and cranially. The 'golden finger' instrument is used to hook intersegmental veins, including the two and four a vein, which is secured with hemolog clips. A buttonhole in the left hepatic vein is identified, and gauze is placed to prevent air embolism. The head of the patient is slightly elevated. The ligamentum venosum is dissected using harmonic, and small veins from the umbilical portion of the portal vein are clipped. The parenchyma around the left hepatic vein is narrowed, and pneumoperitoneum is adjusted to control bleeding.

The surgeon uses a 60 mm white stapler to staple the left hepatic vein, leaving minimal parenchyma posteriorly. The harmonic forceps are then used to take down the remaining parenchyma, completing the left lateral sectionectomy. The transection surface is inspected, showing no bile leak or bleeding, and hemostasis is confirmed. The stapled segment two and three portal pedicle is visible with a tan 45 mm tri-staple. The specimen is extracted through the incision with the aid of a rubber band.
